From the fastest car
on earth. To the world's
biggest toy company.

Scroll
Sound familiar?

Most studios know
your sector.
That is not the same
as knowing your problem.

You have a product people love. But the moment they encounter it in a room, something does not quite connect. The object is right. The space around it feels like it belongs to someone else.

The brand has history. Real history. But somewhere along the way the design stopped keeping pace with what the brand actually stands for. It still looks like the past. It just does not feel like the future.

Everyone who works here believes in what you do. But walk through the door and nothing tells that story. The work is extraordinary. The place that holds it is ordinary.

Different categories. Different scales. The same quality of thought.

What we do

Product. Place. Play.
One team.

Not three disciplines running in parallel. One complete conversation — where the thinking across product, place and play happens together, from the first session.

The brief rarely stays in one lane. A product shapes the space around it. A space changes how a product feels. Play runs through both. Quarterre holds all of it at once, because the studio was built that way.

01
Product

Objects with purpose. Designed for the moment they are picked up — and every moment after.

02
Place

Spaces that do not simply exist — they perform. Every decision a considered one.

03
Play

Systems built around human delight. Where the experience is the product.

How we work

Three directors.
One conversation.
From the start.

The three people you meet in the first session are the three people who do the work. No handoff. No brief passed to a junior team. No dilution between disciplines. The thinking stays connected because the people stay connected.

Most studios divide a project between specialists. Quarterre converges on it. Product, place and play are not handled separately — they are considered together, from the first question to the last decision.

15
Years in practice
3
Points of view
1
Position
Selected work

Different categories.
Different scales.
The same quality of thought.

Start a conversation →
Product · Automotive
McLaren

A hypercar with nowhere to put luggage. The constraint was a fourteen-second window — the moment between the garage and the journey. What was at stake was whether the object felt worthy of the machine it was built for.

Play · Product
LEGO

The world's most recognised toy company needed thinking that moved as fast as the business. The constraint was complexity at scale. What was at stake was whether play could stay at the centre as the organisation grew around it.

Place · Workplace
Play headquarters

An organisation whose mission was play but whose environment said otherwise. The constraint was a building that did not tell the story. What was at stake was whether anyone walking through the door would believe in the work being done inside it.

Product · Mobility
Mobility vehicles

The brief was a vehicle for people who had lost independence. The constraint was engineering that left almost no room for considered design. What was at stake was whether the person using it felt seen — or simply transported.

Who we are

Built to think.
Not to scale.

Three directors. Fifteen years. A shared curiosity that runs across every category they have ever worked in. Not a studio organised around sectors — but around a quality of thinking that travels.

They made a deliberate choice not to grow in the way studios are supposed to grow. Because the thing that makes the work good is the same thing that would be the first casualty of scale. The directors who take the first brief are the ones who resolve it. That has always been the point.

Director One
Product & Automotive
Director Two

Director Three
Play & Systems
The right fit

The right client
doesn't need convincing.
They need a studio that
matches their ambition.

Quarterre's strongest relationships are long ones. Clients return across sectors and across years because they trust the thinking, not just the outcome. The brief evolves. The standard does not.

"If you want a studio to execute your vision, there are excellent ones. If you want a team to help you find a better one — that is a different conversation."

Start here

Don't write
the brief yet.
Bring the ambition.
And it writes itself.

Bring the brief. Or just bring the problem. The best projects rarely start with a complete document — they start with a question that does not quite fit the brief you have written.
